summary: Joe Reis offers practical advice for people starting on Substack, emphasizing that everyone starts from zero regardless of their existing following elsewhere. He stresses that long-form writing requires patience, structured thinking, and persistence through the inevitable lonely periods when engagement is minimal, noting that 90% of people quit too early.
key_points: Your audience from other platforms won't automatically follow you to Substack - plan to start from zero
Long-form writing requires structured thinking and patience, unlike short-form social media posts
90% of people quit Substack after getting little initial engagement
Voice-to-text is an effective tool for overcoming writer's block and getting first drafts done
Using AI to generate content wholesale is a dangerous shortcut that cheats both writer and reader
money_quotes: Writing is thinking, and if you're not actively engaged in the writing process, you're cheating yourself and your readers out of your true expression and voice.
Humility is a far better foundation for growth than entitlement.
This is where most people quit. This is also where real writers separate from the tourists.
Building an audience and engagement are side effects of writing things people want to read.
key_insight: Success on Substack comes not from gaming algorithms or leveraging existing audiences, but from the unglamorous work of showing up consistently to write thoughtful long-form content even when it feels like you're writing into the void.
